title: AI SAFETY MODEL MOE: A MIXTURE-OF-EXPERTS ARCHITECTURE FOR CERTIFIABLE, RISK-AWARE GENERATION
text: <jats:p>Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale efficiently by sending each input to just a few specialists (“experts”). Most safety controls today sit after the model generates text (filters and classifiers), which means unsafe content can still be produced internally and only blocked at the end. This paper proposes Safety-MoE, a design that pushes safety into the architecture: a risk-aware router that uses safety signals to choose experts; an auditor that watches the output as it is being written and can switch to safer experts or stop; and a final gate that either approves the answer or abstains and offers a safe alternative. We formalize safety as a risk functional ℛ(𝑥, 𝑦), train with a utility-vs-risk objective, and show how to calibrate the abstention threshold so the overall system’s risk stays under a target budget 𝜏. Color-coded figure illustrates the architecture and the safety-utility trade-off, and we provide concrete tables, pseudocode, and deployment checklists to make implementation straightforward. Plain-English takeaway: Safety-MoE makes the entire pipeline safety-aware which experts are used, when to switch, and when to bow out so the chance of unsafe output drops before it’s ever produced. See §6.1 for P r( 𝑍 = 1 ∣ 𝑟̂ ≤ 𝜏 ) ≤ 𝜏 + 𝜀guarantee and §6.3 for coverage-risk trade-offs.</jats:p>

title: DEVELOPMENT OF AN AUTOMATION MODEL FOR A MEDICAL DIAGNOSTIC SYSTEM BASED ON THE MIXTURE OF EXPERTS (MOE) ARCHITECTURE
text: <jats:p>Purpose. The article explores architectural approaches to building a medical diagnostic system. The aim of the study is to develop a model for automating diagnostic processes, the application of which enables the creation of a system for the healthcare sector that can effectively complement a physician's expertise by increasing the accuracy and speed of diagnosis through comprehensive analysis of available medical information. Methods. The research is based on Bayesian methods for modeling uncertainty in the diagnostic process. To improve the interpretability of diagnostic decisions, methods of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) are used, namely SHAP which allows to determine the influence of each symptom or indicator on the decision made. The process of integration of ontological knowledge is implemented through the architecture of multilayer preceptrons (MLP) with using of gradient boosting methods. Results. The result of the study is a developed model of a general architecture for a medical system capable of effectively processing both structured and unstructured medical data, ensuring adaptability to various clinical scenarios. Scientific Novelty lies in the development of a model for automating the diagnostic processes in medical systems, the application of which in medical system development enhances the accuracy and speed of disease identification during the processing of symptomatic data and ensures high-quality. Practical Significance lies in the fact that the proposed model serves as the foundation for developing software aimed at the rapid diagnosis of patients and providing well-founded recommendations for their further treatment. Keywords: medical diagnostic system, Mixture of Experts architecture, automation model, symptomatic data, expert.</jats:p>
